article_meta.title: A California judge scolded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team after they showed up to a Los Angeles courtroom weari

reasoning: The model makes one clear attribution rule violation by flagging 'an extraordinary misstep' which is Jacob Ward's characterization (attributed source language). The other two flags are defensible: 'high-stakes' and 'scolded' are author prose choices. However, the model also missed some potential bias instances like 'strike at the heart of the national debate over Big Tech's influence on America's youth' which is author editorializing. The response also has markdown fences despite instructions not to include them.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: 'An extraordinary misstep' is clearly attributed to Jacob Ward ('Ward described the episode as'), making this an attribution rule violation. The other two flagged items ('high-stakes' and 'scolded') are genuinely in the author's own prose.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Two of three items (scolded, high-stakes) are defensible author-prose bias flags. 'An extraordinary misstep' is a false positive due to attribution violation. So 1 of 3 items is clearly wrong.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed 'claims that strike at the heart of the national debate over Big Tech's influence on America's youth' which is dramatic author editorializing (Sensationalism/Emotionalism or Word Choice). 'closely watched trial' is also author editorial framing. These are moderate misses.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: For the correctly identified items: 'scolded' as Word Choice is correct; 'high-stakes trial' as Sensationalism/Emotionalism is defensible. The wrongly-flagged 'extraordinary misstep' was labeled Subjective Qualifying Adjectives which would have been reasonable if it were author prose. Labels are generally appropriate.
